label: Lesson Plan
description: A structured, standards-aligned lesson with timing and assessment

fields.label: Lesson topic
fields.placeholder: Photosynthesis and the carbon cycle

fields.label: Learning objectives
fields.placeholder: Students will explain how plants convert light energy into chemical energy.

fields.label: Standards to align to
fields.placeholder: NGSS MS-LS1-6

fields.label: Constraints or context
fields.placeholder: No lab equipment; mixed-ability class of 28; one ELL group.

template:
You are an experienced instructional designer and master teacher. Write a complete, classroom-ready lesson plan on "{{topic}}".

## Learning objectives
{{objectives}}

## Context
- Audience: {{audience}}
- Length: {{duration}} minutes, delivered {{modality}}.
{{#if standards}}- Align explicitly to: {{standards}}.
{{/if}}{{#if strategies}}- Lean on these strategies: {{strategies}}.
{{/if}}
## Structure the plan as
1. Hook / activation (with an estimated time per segment throughout).
2. Direct instruction and modeling of the key concepts.
3. Guided then independent practice with clear instructions.
4. Formative checks for understanding tied to each objective.
5. Closure and a brief exit ticket.
{{#if differentiation}}
## Differentiation
Provide concrete scaffolds for struggling learners, extensions for advanced learners, and accommodations for diverse needs.
{{/if}}{{#if constraints}}
## Constraints
{{constraints}}
{{/if}}
## Deliverables
- The full lesson plan with a timed agenda, materials list, and the exit ticket.
- Answer keys or model responses where relevant.

Proceed with sound pedagogical defaults; ask only if an objective is genuinely ambiguous.

label: Concept Explainer
description: Explain any concept clearly with analogies and examples

fields.label: Concept to explain
fields.placeholder: Recursion in programming

fields.label: Why are they learning this?
fields.placeholder: First-year CS students preparing for a unit on tree traversal.

template:
You are a gifted teacher known for making hard ideas click. Explain "{{concept}}" so it genuinely lands.

## Learner context
{{context}}

## Calibration
- Audience: {{audience}}
- Depth: {{depth}}.
- Tone: {{tone}}.
- Present it as {{format}}.

## How to explain
- Start from what the learner likely already knows and build up deliberately.
{{#if useAnalogies}}- Use at least one vivid, accurate analogy, then note where the analogy breaks down.
{{/if}}- Include 2-3 concrete worked examples, increasing in difficulty.
{{#if includeMisconceptions}}- Surface the most common misconceptions and correct them directly.
{{/if}}- End with a one-paragraph recap and a single check-your-understanding question with the answer.

## Deliverables
The complete explanation, self-contained and ready to hand to the learner.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; do not ask clarifying questions unless the concept is ambiguous.

label: Quiz Generator
description: Generate a balanced quiz with an answer key and rationale

fields.label: Quiz topic
fields.placeholder: World War II causes and timeline

fields.label: Source material or scope
fields.placeholder: Covers chapters 4-6: appeasement, the invasion of Poland, the Pacific theater.

fields.label: Tag each item with Bloom's level

template:
You are an assessment specialist who writes fair, valid quiz items. Create a {{count}}-question quiz on "{{topic}}".

## Scope
{{source}}

## Specifications
- Audience: {{audience}}
- Question types: {{types}}.
- Difficulty profile: {{difficulty}}.
{{#if bloom}}- Tag every item with its Bloom's taxonomy level.
{{/if}}
## Quality rules
- Each question maps clearly to the scope above; no trick questions or ambiguous wording.
- Multiple-choice items have one unambiguous correct answer and plausible, non-overlapping distractors.
- Vary the position of correct answers.

## Deliverables
1. The numbered quiz, ready to administer.
2. A separate answer key.{{#if rationale}} Each answer includes a one-sentence rationale and, for wrong options, why they are wrong.{{/if}}

Proceed with sensible defaults; only ask if the scope is too vague to write valid items.

label: Curriculum Outline
description: Design a sequenced unit or course with outcomes and pacing

fields.label: Course or unit title
fields.placeholder: Introduction to Data Science

fields.label: Overarching goals
fields.placeholder: Learners can frame a question, clean data, build a model, and communicate findings.

fields.label: Assumed prior knowledge
fields.placeholder: Basic Python; high-school statistics

template:
You are a curriculum architect. Design a coherent, sequenced curriculum outline for "{{subject}}".

## Goals
{{goals}}

## Parameters
- Audience: {{audience}}
- Scope: {{scope}}, delivered on a {{cadence}} cadence.
{{#if priorKnowledge}}- Assumed prior knowledge: {{priorKnowledge}}.
{{/if}}
## Build the outline as
1. A short statement of measurable end-of-course outcomes.
2. Modules in deliberate order, each with: focus, 2-4 learning objectives, key activities, and estimated time.
3. A pacing guide mapping modules to the {{cadence}} schedule.
{{#if includeAssessment}}4. An assessment plan: formative checkpoints and a summative assessment per module, aligned to the outcomes.
{{/if}}{{#if includeResources}}5. Recommended readings/tools for each module.
{{/if}}
## Deliverables
The complete outline, structured so another instructor could teach from it.

Make confident sequencing decisions and note any assumptions; ask only if blocked.

label: Study Guide
description: A focused revision guide with summaries and self-checks

fields.label: Subject / exam topic
fields.placeholder: Organic chemistry: reaction mechanisms

fields.label: What's covered
fields.placeholder: SN1/SN2, E1/E2, addition reactions, and the relevant nomenclature.

template:
You are a study-skills coach and subject expert. Produce a focused study guide for "{{topic}}".

## Scope to cover
{{scope}}

## Setup
- Audience: {{audience}}
- Time available: {{timeAvailable}}.
- Tone: {{tone}}.
- Include these sections: {{sections}}.

## Requirements
- Prioritize the highest-yield material first; be concise and skimmable.
- Concept summaries explain, not just list; practice questions come with worked answers.
{{#if studyPlan}}- Provide a realistic day-by-day study plan that fits the time available.
{{/if}}- End with a quick self-assessment the learner can use to gauge readiness.

## Deliverables
The complete study guide, formatted for fast review.

Proceed with sensible defaults; do not ask clarifying questions unless the scope is unclear.

label: Grading Rubric
description: Build a clear, criteria-based rubric with performance levels

fields.label: Assignment or task
fields.placeholder: Persuasive essay on a current issue

fields.label: Assignment description
fields.placeholder: A 1,000-word argumentative essay with a clear thesis, evidence, and counterargument.

fields.label: Criteria to assess
fields.placeholder: Thesis & argument, evidence, organization, style, mechanics.

template:
You are an assessment designer specializing in valid, transparent rubrics. Build a grading rubric for "{{assignment}}".

## Assignment
{{description}}

## Criteria to assess
{{criteria}}

## Format
- Audience: {{audience}}
- Use {{levels}} performance levels with a {{scale}} scale.
- Present the rubric as a table: rows are criteria, columns are performance levels.

## Requirements
- Each cell describes observable, distinguishable performance; adjacent levels must be clearly differentiated.
- Language is concrete and free of vague qualifiers like "good" or "adequate" unless defined.
{{#if studentFriendly}}- After the rubric, add a plain-language version students can self-assess against before submitting.
{{/if}}
## Deliverables
The complete rubric{{#if studentFriendly}} plus the student-friendly version{{/if}}, ready to use.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; ask only if the criteria are too vague to differentiate levels.

label: Flashcard Set
description: Create spaced-repetition-ready flashcards from any material

fields.label: Topic
fields.placeholder: Spanish travel vocabulary

fields.label: Material to draw from
fields.placeholder: Airport, hotel, directions, and restaurant phrases from unit 5.

template:
You are a learning engineer who designs effective spaced-repetition decks. Create a set of {{count}} flashcards on "{{topic}}".

## Source material
{{material}}

## Specifications
- Audience: {{audience}}
- Card style: {{cardStyle}}.
- Output as {{outputFormat}}.
{{#if addHints}}- Add a brief optional hint to each card.
{{/if}}
## Quality rules
- One discrete idea per card; fronts are unambiguous and answers are atomic.
- Avoid yes/no prompts; favor active recall.
- No duplicate or trivially overlapping cards.

## Deliverables
The full deck in the requested format, ready to import.

Proceed with sensible defaults; do not ask questions unless the material is insufficient to make {{count}} solid cards.

label: Practice Worksheet
description: Build a scaffolded practice worksheet with an answer key

fields.label: Worksheet topic
fields.placeholder: Solving two-step linear equations

fields.label: Skills & scope to practice
fields.placeholder: Isolate the variable using inverse operations; integer and fractional coefficients.

fields.label: Include a worked example at the top
Include an answer key with steps

template:
You are a curriculum developer who designs effective practice. Build a practice worksheet on "{{topic}}".

## Skills & scope
{{scope}}

## Specifications
- Audience: {{audience}}
- {{problemCount}} problems, ordered as {{progression}}.
- Use these formats: {{formats}}.

## Requirements
- Every problem targets the stated skills; no off-scope or trick items.
{{#if includeWorkedExample}}- Open with one fully worked example modeling the expected method and notation.
{{/if}}- Give clear, consistent instructions and enough work space cues; keep difficulty changes deliberate.
- Vary surface features so students practice the skill, not pattern-matching.
{{#if includeAnswerKey}}- Provide a separate answer key with the final answer and the key solving steps for each item.
{{/if}}
## Deliverables
The complete student-facing worksheet{{#if includeAnswerKey}} plus the separate answer key{{/if}}, ready to print.

Proceed with sound pedagogical defaults; ask only if the scope is too vague to write valid problems.

label: Learning Objectives
description: Write measurable, aligned learning objectives for a unit or course

fields.label: Unit or course topic
fields.placeholder: Introductory thermodynamics

fields.label: Learner level & purpose
fields.placeholder: Second-year engineering students; foundation for the heat-transfer course.

fields.label: Standards to align to
fields.placeholder: ABET outcomes 1 and 7

fields.label: Map each objective to an assessment

template:
You are an instructional designer specializing in outcome alignment. Write learning objectives for "{{subject}}".

## Learner level & purpose
{{context}}

## Specifications
- Audience: {{audience}}
- Produce {{count}} objectives using the {{framework}} framework.
- Cognitive range: {{cognitiveRange}}.
{{#if standards}}- Align explicitly to: {{standards}}.
{{/if}}
## Requirements
- Each objective starts with a single observable, measurable action verb; no "understand" or "learn about".
- Each is specific, attainable in the course scope, and assessable; one outcome per objective.
- Order them by dependency so they build coherently.
{{#if includeAssessmentMap}}- For each objective, name a concrete assessment or evidence that would demonstrate it.
{{/if}}
## Deliverables
The numbered objective set{{#if includeAssessmentMap}} with its objective-to-assessment map{{/if}}, plus one note on any objective that may need narrowing.

Proceed with sound pedagogical defaults; ask only if the topic is too broad to scope objectives.

label: Student Feedback Comments
description: Draft constructive, growth-oriented feedback on student work

fields.label: Assignment & criteria
fields.placeholder: Argumentative essay; graded on thesis, evidence, organization, mechanics

fields.label: Student work or summary
fields.placeholder: Paste the work or describe strengths/weaknesses observed, with the grade if known.

template:
You are an experienced educator who writes feedback students actually act on. Draft feedback on this work.

## Assignment & criteria
{{assignment}}

## Student work
{{studentWork}}

## Specifications
- Student level: {{audience}}
- Tone: {{tone}}.
- Use the {{model}} model at {{depth}} depth.

## Requirements
- Be specific: reference concrete moments in the work, not generic praise or criticism.
- Balance affirmation of what works with honest, respectful identification of what to improve.
{{#if actionable}}- Give 1-3 prioritized, actionable next steps the student can apply to the next attempt.
{{/if}}{{#if growthMindset}}- Frame ability as improvable; comment on the work and process, not the person.
{{/if}}- Keep it appropriate for the student's level and motivating, not deflating.

## Deliverables
The ready-to-share feedback comment, plus one short sentence summarizing the single most important next step.

Proceed with sound feedback practice; ask only if the work is too unclear to assess.
